title: Cutting tiles with a scorer and nipper
subtitle: Score, snap, and nibble ceramic tiles to size without a wet saw
excerpt: How to cut ceramic wall tiles with a tile scorer, tile nipper, and a straight-edge: scoring a clean line, snapping it over a batten, and nibbling curves and notches for pipes and sockets.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Standards BS 5385-1.

glossaryTerms.definition: A handheld tool with a tungsten carbide or steel wheel that scores a groove across the glazed face of a ceramic tile, creating a fracture line along which the tile can be snapped cleanly.
A plier-like tool with two hardened carbide jaws used to nibble small irregular bites from the edge of a ceramic tile for curves, pipe holes, and notched cut-outs.

content.text: A
tile scorer
and a
tile nipper

handle the majority of cuts on a bathroom tiling job without the cost of a wet tile saw. Straight cuts across a tile snap cleanly if the score is firm and continuous. Notched cuts around pipes and sockets require the nipper. A wet saw gives a cleaner edge and is worth hiring for large-format tiles or porcelain, but most standard ceramic bathroom tiles cut perfectly well by hand.
What you need
Tile scorer.
A simple pencil-style scorer with a tungsten carbide wheel. Check the wheel is not chipped before use.
Tile nipper.
For curves and notches.
Steel straight-edge or a purpose-made tile-cutting guide for straight cuts.
A scrap of timber to snap tiles over, and a piece of foam or rubber to protect the tile face.
Tile file or carborundum stone for smoothing cut edges.
Safety glasses. A snapped tile edge is sharp.
Straight cuts: score and snap
Step 1, mark the cut line.
Measure the gap to be filled, subtract 2 mm for the grout joint, and mark the cut line on the glazed face of the tile with a pencil. Place the tile on a firm flat surface.
Step 2, score in one continuous pass.

Hold the straight-edge against the pencil line. Apply firm, even pressure to the scorer wheel and draw it across the tile in a single pass from edge to edge. Hear the glaze crunch slightly as the wheel cuts. Do not go back and forth: a double score creates two competing fracture lines and the tile will break unpredictably. One firm pass is all the tile needs.
Step 3, snap over a batten.

Place a 25 x 25 mm batten or a pencil under the tile, aligned with the score line. Press down firmly and evenly on both sides of the score simultaneously with both palms. The tile snaps along the score line. The snap must be fast and decisive; a slow press tends to crack the tile through the body rather than along the score.
Notched cuts: using the nipper

Mark the notch or curve on the tile face. Make two straight cuts with the scorer to define the sides of the notch where they meet the edge of the tile, then snap those cuts. Use the nipper to take small bites from the waste area, working progressively toward the marked line. Never try to bite more than 5 mm at a time; large bites crack the tile. Work toward the line in steps, then smooth the edge with the tile file.
Pipe holes

A pipe hole in the centre of a tile requires a drill with a carbide or diamond-tipped tile drill bit. Mark the centre of the hole with a cross. Start the drill at low speed without hammer action until the bit has a purchase through the glaze, then increase speed. Do not use the hammer function on ceramic; it shatters the tile. For a half-hole where the pipe emerges at a tile joint, score a semicircle on each of the two tiles with the nipper and join them around the pipe.
What a correct result looks like

A clean straight cut has a uniform fracture face with no jagged sections or flaking from the glaze. The cut edge can be sharpened to glass-smooth with two passes of a tile file. A nipper cut is rougher but should follow the marked line closely. The cut edge is always positioned facing into the room corner, behind the silicone or grout joint, where it is concealed.
Common mistakes
Scoring partway across the tile.
The tile snaps from the end of the score, not along the full line. Always score from one edge to the other in a single stroke.
Taking too large a bite with the nipper.
A bite wider than 5 mm extends the crack into the body of the tile. Work in small increments.
Measuring too tight.
Cut tiles need 2 mm less than the measured gap to allow for the grout joint and give a small tolerance for the tile going in slightly off-square.
